EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Lowndes County teen was struck by lightning while outside his home.  Three days later, he died of cardiac failure at Shands Hospital in Gainesville.  Reported by the Valdosta Daily Times.
A home was gutted by fire as a result of a lightning strike.  Reported by the Berrien County-Nashville Press.
Several downed trees along Highway 112 reported by the Turner County Sheriff.
Lightning struck a 300-foot emergency communications tower, which knocked the primary and backup 911 systems offline for 20 hours.  Reported by the Albany Herald.
Heavy rain caused flooded roads.
Heavy rainfall flooded streets leaving at least one car inundated by water.
Heavy rainfall flooded numerous roads including Commerce Street and Heritage Drive in Hinesville and Cassells Road in Flemington.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and power lines.
Very heavy rainfall flooded many roads including South Main Street, Bacon Road, and Easy Street.  Several vehicles were trapped in the water and a house was flooded.
Due to very heavy rainfall, 2 miles of Old Groveland Road were under water.
Heavy rainfall flooded U.S. Route 301 and several other roads near Glennville.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down large limbs across power lines.

The Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that up to four inches of rain had fallen in parts of the county during the day, resulting in street flooding at a number of locations around the county.

The News and Farmer-Jefferson Reporter of Louisville reported that thunderstorm winds, associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill, caused considerable damage to a home west of Louisville on Grange Road.  A portion of the roof over the garage collapsed.  The house also sustained other minor damage.  A number of trees were blown down or split in half across the street and even up to one-third of a mile away.  Several residents in the area reported seeing a tornado that was approximately 10 to 15 feet wide and traveled about 60 to 75 feet above the ground.   One resident about four miles west of Louisville reported seeing two tornadoes.

The Jasper county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable.   The Monticello News reported that several roads had to be closed, including Meadows, Pitts Chapel, Shepherd, Faulkner, Millen, and Cook Roads.

Gwinnett county SkyWarn spotters and the county 911 center reported that an extensive number of roads across the county were flooded.  Several roads were impassable and a number had been closed.  Lench-Candler Road in Lawrenceville had up to a foot of water flowing over it and was closed.  Rock House Road, also in Lawrenceville, had water over it as well and was also closed.
The Jasper county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down.  One tree fell on a carport, destroying the carport.
The Walton county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable.
The Greene county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable.

A National Weather Official river gage showed that Peachtree Creek had risen above the flood stage of 17.0 feet, with a stage of 17.8 feet recorded.  A number of roads and homes adjacent to the creek were flooded.

A National Weather Official river gage showed that Peachtree Creek had risen above the flood stage of 17.0 feet, with a stage of 17.8 feet recorded.  A number of roads and homes adjacent to the creek were flooded.  In addition, a log jam of water against  the Cheshire Bridge on La Vista Road caused the road to be impassable and closed.
Large tree limbs were blown down on Aikens Bridge Rd.
Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.
Large tree limbs were blown down on highway 105 near the Stephens County line.
The Coweta county 911 center reported that one power line was down at the intersection of Fisher Road and Lower Fayetteville Road.
The Covington Police Department reported that nearly 4 inches of rain fell during the day resulting in minor street flooding throughout the city.

The Forest Blade of Swainsboro reported that thunderstorm winds associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill uprooted several trees onto a car and house on Pinetucky Road near Kite.  Some residents in the area reported seeing a funnel cloud.

The public reported that a number of trees had been blown down.   Atlanta TV Channel 46 reported that one tree fell on a church on Clairmont road in Chamblee, causing damage to the structure.  The county 911 center later reported that some power lines were down in the Stone Mountain area.  The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that in Decatur, a very large oak tree fell on and crushed a car with two occupants inside.  However, no injuries were reported with this incident.
The Carroll county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.
Very heavy rainfall caused several creeks to rapidly overflow their banks and flood adjacent roads. An overtopped lake also caused some flooding of Wilson Rd.
A slow-moving thunderstorm produced very heavy rainfall, which cuased a section of highway 76 to flood in the Persimmon Community.

The Barrow county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded, impassable, and closed.  In addition, a drainage spillway in Auburn was submerged, but the dam remained in good condition.  The Heartland Retirement Center also suffered some damage from flooding.
The Jackson county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded, impassable, and closed.
A trained storm spotter reported that a number of trees had been either blown down, uprooted, or twisted off near Georgia Highway 155 about two miles south of Georgia Highway 138.
The Jackson county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.

A storm damage survey conducted by an employee of the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia and reports from the Emergency Management Director, storm spotters, and the public confirmed that an F1 tornado touched down about 3 miles north-northeast of Pennington near Spears Road and just west of Georgia Highway 83, also known as Monticello Road.  The tornado continued on a sporadic path for approximately 3 miles, terminating near Interstate-20, about four miles southwest of Madison.  The tornado was associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill and occurred just east of the center of the circulation associated with the remnants of the tropical storm.  The Morgan county Emergency Management Director reported that extensive damage occurred to a dairy farm on Spears Road.  Two dairy sheds were severely damaged, a 50 by 50 foot hay barn was destroyed, a 30 by 60 foot feed barn and a John Deere Tractor were significantly damaged, a two-car carport was destroyed as well as a Chevrolet Tahoe that was parked inside, and three other approximately 8 by 12 foot metal storage buildings were severely damaged or destroyed.  One dairy cow was also killed by a falling tree.  Seven other homes along Spears Road, Monticello Highway, and Brownwood Road sustained minor to moderate damage during the incident, mostly roof damage.  One of the homes had half of its roof shingles blown off.  One home sustained damage to several windows, and another residence had both a utility trailer and a car damaged by fallen trees. At an eighth location, a metal outbuilding sustained damage.  The damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service found that hundreds of trees were blown down along the path of the tornado in the heavily forested area west of Georgia Highway 83 and southwest of Madison.
A couple of trees were blown down.

A storm survey conducted by an employee of the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, Georgia determined that approximately 30 or more isolated trees were blown down by straight line winds on Monticello Road, Clack Road, and Spears Road.  The survey determined these downed trees to be separate from the F1 tornado damage that occurred later.
A storm spotter reported that a number of roads in the Chamblee area were flooded and impassable.

The Forest Blade of Swainsboro reported that residents in the area sighted funnel clouds.  Damage to trees and property was reported in the area and additional funnel cloud reports were received only 10 minutes later further north in Jefferson county along with additional damage to trees and property.
The Dalton Citizen reported that heavy rain, associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill, forced the closing of Patricia Avenue for the third time in six weeks.
The public reported quarter-sized hail.

The public reported that at least five medium-sized trees were blown down.  One of the trees landed on a brand new pickup truck and boat.  The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that power lines were blown down across cars on Tara Boulevard in the Morrow area.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported that several tree limbs were blown down.  Wind gusts were estimated at 50 mph.  Pea-sized hail was also reported.
The Clarke county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down.
The public reported that a number of roads in the area were covered with flowing water, several inches deep.

The Calhoun police department reported that thunderstorm winds blew part of a roof off an apartment building, tore down a couple of traffic signals, and blew down a number of trees.  Most of this damage occurred along and near Georgia Highway 156, Redbud Road in Calhoun.

The News and Farmer-Jefferson Reporter of Louisville reported that several residents saw at least two separate funnel clouds, almost touching the ground, in the area.  The reports originated from U.S. Highway 221, about 4 miles southwest of Louisville.  Damage to trees and property was reported in the area.

The Fulton county 911 center reported several times during the afternoon that heavy rainfall caused Peachtree Creek to be forced quickly above flood stage of 17 feet.  Cheshire Bridge on La Vista Road was flooded and impassible, largely caused by a log jam of water against the bridge.  Several roads and some homes along and near the creek were also flooded during the incident.
A prolonged period of moderate to heavy rainfall resulted in eventual flooding of several roads in the Clarkesville area.
At least one mud slide developed in the Yonah Dam vicinity due to excessive rainfall. A few roads were also flooded due to overflowing creeks in this same area.
A few roads flooded, particularly Robinson Mountain Rd near Mountain City.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The Barrow county 911 center reported that two trees had been blown down.

The public, as well as the local Gainesville newspaper, reported that a number of trees, some very large, were blown down around the county, especially in the Oakwood and Flowery Branch areas.  Several power lines were knocked down by the fallen trees.  Two homes in the southern part of the county sustained damage when large trees, one of them 65-feet tall, fell on the homes.  Other reports from the public indicated that shingles were removed from the the roofs of some homes.   Over 7000 people in the county were left without power for several hours following the thunderstorm.

This weak tornado caused damage to the roof of one building and blew the roof off an outbuilding along highway 106. Numerous trees and large tree limbs were also blown down along its intermittent 4-mile path.
This weak tornado blew down numerous limbs and trees along its intermittent 11-mile track. Part of a metal roof was blown off a building near Toccoa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: County emergency managers reported that strong non-convective winds blew down several trees.  The winds were in association with a strong low-level jet in advance of a cold front and accompanying line of rain showers.  Wind gusts in Forsyth county were estimated at 50 mph by a storm spotter.  Sustained winds of 25 to 30 mph, with gusts of 35 to 40 mph, were common throughout the area for a brief period.

County emergency managers reported that strong non-convective winds blew down several trees.  The winds were in association with a strong low-level jet in advance of a cold front and accompanying line of rain showers.  Wind gusts in Forsyth county were estimated at 50 mph by a storm spotter.  Sustained winds of 25 to 30 mph, with gusts of 35 to 40 mph, were common throughout the area for a brief period.

High winds developed ahead of a cold front across the mountains of northeast Georgia, mainly in the higher elevations. Several trees were blown down across the area. In northern Habersham County, a tractor trailer collided with a fallen tree.

High winds developed ahead of a cold front across the mountains of northeast Georgia, mainly in the higher elevations. Several trees were blown down across the area. In northern Habersham County, a tractor trailer collided with a fallen tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed behind a cold front across the mountains of northeast Georgia. Sustained winds of 40 mph developed during the pre-dawn hours and persisted most of the day, especially in the highest elevations. Several trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Douglas county 911 center reported that strong non-convective winds blew down some signs and blew an awning off a building in the Douglasville area.
The Walton county 911 center reported that strong non-convective winds blew down some trees around the county.
The Laurens county Emergency Managment Director reported penny-sized hail.
The Walker county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down in the area causing some minor power outages.
The Dade county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down on Porter Road west of Stephensville.
The Oconee county Emergency Manager reported that a number of trees and several power lines had been blown down.

A storm spotter for the National Weather Service reported that a poorly, yet recently constructed building housing a graphic printing company, collapsed during a heavy rain event.  The heavy rain caused the poorly anchored concrete support pillars to collapse.  The building was a total loss.
The public reported that strong winds, associated with a strong cold front, had blown down numerous trees and power lines across the county.
The Gwinnett Daily-Post of Lawrenceville reported that a large, 60-foot tree fell on a Toyata, crushing the vehicle, but leaving the person inside uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Bill, which was earlier Tropical Storm Bill, tracked across north and central Georgia during the day bringing heavy rain, flooding, wind damage, and even an isolated tornado to the region.  The storm, which formed in the Gulf of Mexico Sunday morning, June 29th, moved inland between New Orleans, Louisiana and Mobile, Alabama on Monday June 30th, then tracked northeast to near Tuscaloosa, Alabama by the morning of July 30th, then turned east-northeast and accelerated.  The depression moved between Birmingham, Alabama and Atlanta, Georgia during the afternoon of July 1st, exiting northeast Georgia after midnight on July 2nd.  Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local newspaper of Cochran reported that lightning struck and set on fire a barn on Cary Road.  The barn was a complete loss.

The Bibb county Sheriff and the Macon Police Department reported that heavy rain over a one to two hour period had resulted in minor street flooding, as well as minor flooding of the yards of some homes.  There were a few roads in the Macon area that had one or two lanes closed for a while.

The Gilmer county 911 center reported that several creeks in the eastern portion of the county, particularly Big Creek, had risen well out of their banks as a thunderstorm with torrential rainfall persisted over the area during the evening.  Big Creek Road southwest of Big Creek was flooded and closed, along with several homes in the area southwest of Big Creek.
The Whitfield county 911 center reported that three roads were flooded in the area with water flowing over the roads.  All of these roads were still blocked by flood waters at midnight EDT.
The Butts county 911 center reported that a few trees had been blown down.
The Polk county 911 center reported that heavy rain had resulted in minor street flooding in the Aragon and Rockmart areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down.
Some trees were blown down in the Canon area.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported penny to quarter-sized hail.

The Banks county 911 center reported that excessive rainfall from repetitive thunderstorm activity had resulted in several creeks overflowing their banks and flowing across roads.  Some of the roads had been rendered impassable because of the flowing water.
The Madison county 911 center reported that a few trees had been blown down.
The Muscogee county 911 center reported that lightning struck a tree near a house.  The tree subsequently fell on the house causing damage to the structure.

The United States Post Office in Calhoun reported that several roads in the city were flooded with around one foot of water on them.  Mail couriers in the area were having difficulty navagating the flooded roadways.

The Banks county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down between Homer and Hollingsworth along U.S. Highway 441.  Three roads were blocked and closed because of the downed trees.  A number of power lines had also been brought down by the fallen trees.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

The Jackson county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down in Commerce and a utility pole was down on an 18-wheel truck on Interstate-85 just north of Commerce.  A few other trees were also down in the same general area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that a convenience store at the intersection of Georgia Highways 136 and 151 suffered considerable damage as flood waters rose several inches deep inside the building.  The flooding was the result of a small, yet very slow moving thunderstorm with torrential rainfall of up to three inches per hour.

Numerous reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to golf balls, were received from the public.  Golf ball-sized hail was reported in Cassville, quarter-sized hail in White, and penny to half-dollar-sized hail in Cartersville.
The public reported that a tree was blown down at a private residence, causing damage to an adjacent fence.
Several reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the public.
An amateur radio operator reported that lightning set a house on fire just southwest of Fayetteville in the Lakemont subdivision.
The Wilkinson county 911 center reported that three trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dawson News and Advertiser reported that lightning strikes caused several fires around the county, especially the western portion, and killed a rare white Siberian Tiger at a local zoo.  Two homes were struck and set on fire by lightning, one being a total loss in the Big Canoe area where a small forest fire also erupted from another lightning strike.  Another home was set on fire in Dawsonville, which sustained minor damage and a gas line was also struck near another home.
The Dodge county 911 center reported that several trees were down on the roads.
The public reported quarter-sized hail while the Chattahoochee county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Dade county Assistant 911 Director reported that a couple of small trees were down.  He estimated wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph.
The Walker county 911 center reported that trees had been blown down in several areas across the county.
The Walker county 911 center and the public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported penny to nickel-sized hail.
The Baldwin county 911 center reported that several trees had been blown down and some power lines were down as well.
The Baldwin county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Morgan county 911 center reported that several roadways in town were flooded with water flowing over the roads.
The public and the Chattooga county 911 center reported that a number of trees were down, including several large trees which were down across Georgia Highway 48.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cave Springs Fire Department reported that several trees were blown down in Cave Springs and the Floyd county 911 center reported that several trees were blown down in Armuchee.  The Rome New Tribune reported that a number of trees and power lines were down throughout the county.  Cleanup crews worked for hours to clear debris from roads.  Over 5000 people were left without power for several hours following the thunderstorm.

The Bartow county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down throughout the county.  In Cartersville, the public reported that one tree fell across their front porch, causing damage to the structure.
The Polk county 911 center reported that several trees were down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Bill, which was earlier Tropical Storm Bill, tracked across north and central Georgia during the day bringing heavy rain, flooding, wind damage, and even an isolated tornado to the region.  The storm, which formed in the Gulf of Mexico Sunday morning, June 29th, moved inland between New Orleans, Louisiana and Mobile, Alabama on Monday June 30th, then tracked northeast to near Tuscaloosa, Alabama by the morning of July 30th, then turned east-northeast and accelerated.  The depression moved between Birmingham, Alabama and Atlanta, Georgia during the afternoon of July 1st, exiting northeast Georgia after midnight on July 2nd.  Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were blown down.
The Coweta county 911 center, as well as the public, reported that a number of trees were blown down.

The Emanuel county 911 center reported that several trees were blown down on Georgia Highway 57 and other roads on the west side of town.  Some roads were temporarily blocked by the downed trees.
The Dougals county 911 center reported that two trees were down near Sweetwater Creek because of very heavy rain and wind gusts to around 35 mph.
The Americus Times-Recorder reported that one tree was blown down on Lee Street, blocking the road for a brief period until crews could remove it.
The Gilmer county 911 center reported that a number of trees were down throughout the county.
The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that lightning struck a barn on Old Federal road and set it on fire.  The barn was a total loss.

The Sandersville Fire Department and the county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down in the city.  The public reported that a Chevron Gasoline Station sign had been blown down onto a vehicle.  The Sandersville Progress newspaper of Washington county reported that thunderstorm winds blew a large tree down onto a home on Elizabeth Street in Sandersville, causing the roof on the back of the home to cave in.
The Lumpkin county 911 center reported that at least five trees had been blown down.
The Lumpkin county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.

The depression moved between Birmingham, Alabama and Atlanta, Georgia during the afternoon of July 1st, exiting northeast Georgia after midnight on July 2nd.  Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

The Dawson county Emergency Management Director reported that excessive rainfall had forced several creeks out of their banks across the western portion of the county near Juno. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that Kennesaw Avenue was flooded, with up to two feet of water over the road in spots between Kennesaw and Marietta.  Others reported that some roads in the Six Flags area of far south Cobb county were flooded with several inches of water flowing over the roads.
Local Atlanta television news media reported that a number of roads were flooded across the western portion of the county.
An amateur radio operator reported that one tree was blown down on a house in downtown Jonesboro and another tree was blown down on a car.
An amateur radio operator reported that heavy rain resulted in flooding of U.S. Highway 29 in Palmetto.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported penny to quarter-sized hail in Eton.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

The  Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck several buildings and homes just off Arnold Mill Road.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.  Pea-sized hail was reported 5-6 miles north of Woodstock.

The Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that a number of trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds.  One tree fell through the roof and part of the Neel Law Firm Building and another tree fell down on the historic Roselawn property.  Other trees had been blown down on roads in the area.  Trees were also blown down at the court house and the nearby health buliding, as well as in the yards of several residences in the city.  Some power lines were down also and several power outages were reported in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb county 911 center, the public, and the Marietta Daily Journal all reported that a number of trees and power lines had been blown down throughout the county, but especially between Acworth, Kennesaw, and throughout the Marietta area.  A number of trees were down on roads, including Lower Roswell Road and Allgood Road.  A home sustained significant damage on Jim Owens Road near the Kennesaw/Acworth border and another tree was down on a house at a residence on Allgood Road in Marietta.  Two small private planes were pulled free from their tie downs at McCollum field in Marietta and flipped onto their backs.  Both planes sustained significant damage as a result.  A woman, who was 9-months pregnant at the time, suffered minor injuries when a tree struck the windshield of her Sport Utility Vehicle (SUV).  Dobbins Air Force Base, just southeast of Marietta, recorded a wind gust of 66 miles per hour at 136 pm EDT.  At least 5000 homes around the county were left without power for a number of hours.
The Paulding county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down throughout the county.  The public reported that some trees were down across Poplar Springs Road in Hiram.

The Douglas county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down throughout the Douglasville area.  The public reported that two trees were down near the Douglas/Carroll county border, just east of Villa Rica.  The Douglas County Sentinel reported that a home on Hollis Street was partially crushed when a large tree fell on the house.  Although the occupant was at home during the event, he did not suffer any injuries.

WGCL-TV of Atlanta reported that lightning struck a tree in Kennesaw, which subsequently split and fell down.  The Marietta Daily Journal reported that lightning struck several homes in the county causing minor damage.
A wind gust of 51 knots was indicated in the METAR observation for the Columbus (CSG) Airport ASOS.
The Macon Telegraph reported that lightning struck two homes and one business.  Both of the homes suffered extensive damage, while just minor damage to a transformer was reported at the business.
The Whitfield county 911 center reported that several roads were flooded, along with several buildings in the city.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that several trees were down near the Murray county line, just east of Petersburg, near the intersection of  U.S. Highway 411 and Georgia Highway 136.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilkes county Emergency Management Director reported that a number of large trees were blown down or uprooted, many of which fell on power lines.  Other trees were blocking roads.  Power outages were widespread in the area.  One large uprooted tree fell on a house and caused significant damage to the structure.  The Emergency Management Director reported that the damage appeared to be a fairly consistent path from Washington to Metasville and on into Lincoln county.  However, the damage was not indicative of a tornadic circulation.
The Oglethorpe Echo of Lexington reported that thunderstorm winds blew a number of trees down around the county.  Main Street in Lexington was blocked when a large tree fell across the street.

The Georgia Post of Roberta reported that three inches of rain in a short period of time resulted in the erosion of two roads, Blasingame Road and Taylor Mill Road, as swift running water washed over the roads.
The Putnam county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down on Georgia Highway 16 just west of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Waterloch Court.  No one was home at the time and damage was light.

The Polk county 911 center reported that very heavy rain caused flooding of Georgia Highway 27 in Cedartown near the Wal Mart in the Lake Creek area.  Several vehicles became stalled in the high water.  There were also several roads in the city that were flooded.
The Laurens Emergency Management Director and the county 911 center reported that five trees were blown down in Minter and power lines were blown down in Dublin.
The Morgan county Emergency Management Director reported penny to quarter-sized hail in Bostwick and penny-sized hail in Madison.

The Morgan county Emergency Management Director reported that a number of trees and power lines were blown down and that it appeared as if a downburst or microburst had hit around the 1200 block of Nolan Store Road.
The public reported that three trees were blown down on U.S. Highway 441 near Farmington, blocking the road.
The Greene county 911 center reported that around five trees had been blown down.
The Oglethorpe county 911 center reported that a few trees had been blown down.
The Dawson county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down.
The Lumpkin county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down.
The White county 911 center reported that two trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett county SkyWarn network, along with county 911 center and the public reported that a number of trees and tree limbs were blown down from Suwanee, to Lawrenceville, to Snellville.  The Lawrenceville area appeared to be among the hardest hit areas.  One home was damaged in Suwanee and several power lines were blown down around Lawrenceville.  The Forsyth County News reported that a very large tree fell on a home in Barrett Downs and that numerous trees and power lines were down in the area.  The newspaper also reported that trees fell on and injured two people, but no fatalities were reported.  Tree debris, mailboxes, and garbage cans were found on some streets following the storm.
The Newton county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down across the north part of Covington.
An amateur radio operator reported that a couple of trees were blown down.

Numerous reports of hail were received from the public in several areas of the county.  The public reported golf ball-sized hail in Fairmount, quarter-sized hail in Calhoun and southwest of Calhoun, with penny-sized hail east of Calhoun, southwest of Calhoun and just east of Petersburg.
The Pickens county 911 center reported penny to golf ball-sized hail.
The Webster county 911 center reported that several roads in the county were flooded and impassable.
The Murray county 911 center reported that several roads in the south part of the county were flooded with water flowing over the roads.
The public reported quarter-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that from 50 to 75 large trees were blown down or snapped off near Walnut Grove and the county 911 center reported that a large number of trees and several power lines were down in the Monroe area.   Many of these trees were 70 to 80 feet tall and 2  to 2.5 feet in diameter.
The Banks county 911 center reported that at least 23 trees and seven power lines were down across the county.
The Jackson county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe county Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail in Forsyth and across the north part of the county as well.
The public reported that a couple of trees fell, one on a house northeast of Forsyth causing damage both to the roof and the side of the house, while another tree fell across the road.
The public reported that two large trees were blown down across their driveway in the Cherokee Hills Subdivision destroying a vehicle.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that lightning struck and set on fire a laundry room.  Damage was reported to be minor.
The Warrenton Clipper reported that lightning struck and destroyed the chimney on a home, along with two television sets and some telephones inside.

Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton county Emergency Management Director, as well as the Atlanta-Journal Constitution, reported that a large, 100-year old oak tree fell on a Sport Utility Vehicle (SUV) stopped in traffic in the Virginia-Highland neighborhood of midtown Atlanta.  Three of the four people in the vehicle were killed instantly when the tree fell on the vehicle, including a 37-year old mother and her two boys, one three years old and the other only six months old.   The husband in the front seat was uninjured.  There were several other reports of trees and even some power lines down during the thunderstorm which left up to 30,000 residents of Atlanta without power during the evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that a large tree limb fell on a man's car during an early morning  thunderstorm.  The man was killed instantly during the incident as the car was crushed under the weight of the tree limb.

The Jackson News Argus reported that a large 150 year-old Red Oak tree was uprooted during a thunderstorm.  The tree fell on a 1992 Buick, destroying the vehicle, and also caused damage to the garage and part of the house.  A cat, hiding underneath the vehicle, was also killed during the event.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down just southwest of Hartsfield.  It downed several trees and power lines,and destroyed a few mobile homes.  Three barns were damaged.  A truck was totaled by a fallen tree.  A trampoline was wrapped around a pine tree.  Reported by the Colquitt County EMA.
The Catoosa county 911 center reported that several trees were blown down on the south side of Ringgold and across the south part of the county.
The Catoosa county 911 center reported nickel-sized hail on the south side of Ringgold.

The Macon county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were blown down in Montezuma and the National Weather Cooperative observer in Marshallville reported that several power lines were down there.
The public reported that some roads in the Clinton area were flooded and impassable.
The public reported that several trees were uprooted or blown over.
The public reported quarter-sized hail.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton county 911 center reported that there was a two-mile wide swath of damage to trees and some buildings in the Alpharetta area.  Some roads were closed, including Coleman Road which remained closed into the next day.  Fire Station Number 1 in Alpharetta was damaged when three trees fell on and damaged the roof of the building.  Also in Alpharetta, the Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that a pine tree fell on and crushed an unoccupied Jeep on Old Milton Parkway.  Further south in the county, damage was less significant, but nonetheless there were several trees blown down on West Paces Ferry Road in the Buckhead community.  At the Lakewood Antiques Market a 100 year-old red oak fell damaging a new hurricane fence and narrowly missing a workman driving a Bobcat.  The tree was measured at 21 feet in circumference and was 77 feet tall, with an estimated weight of 9 tons.  In the far northern end of the county, around Birmingham, several tree limbs were also blown down.  Well over 100,000 people in the Atlanta Metropolitan area were left without power during the event, with nearly 45,000 homes and businesses still without power the next morning.

The Forsyth county 911 center reported that a number of trees had been blown down across the county.   The Forsyth County news reported that one large tree fell on a home in Barrett Downs, causing considerable damage to the home and two people received minor injuries when trees fell on them.  The Atlanta Journal-Constitution also reported that part of the roof was blown off a Wendy's Restaurant on McFarland Drive.

The Floyd county 911 center reported that several roads were flooded in the northeast part of the county.  Several apartments at the Creekview Estates in Rome were flooded for the third time since March from a nearby swollen creek.  Rainfall up to five inches was recorded in the area during the afternoon.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable.
The Emanuel county 911 center reported that several streets were flooded, impassable, and had to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Bill, which was earlier Tropical Storm Bill, tracked across north and central Georgia during the day bringing heavy rain, flooding, wind damage, and even an isolated tornado to the region.  The storm, which formed in the Gulf of Mexico Sunday morning, June 29th, moved inland between New Orleans, Louisiana and Mobile, Alabama on Monday June 30th, then tracked northeast to near Tuscaloosa, Alabama by the morning of July 30th, then turned east-northeast and accelerated.  The depression moved between Birmingham, Alabama and Atlanta, Georgia during the afternoon of July 1st, exiting northeast Georgia after midnight on July 2nd.  Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees and power lines at Lake Park and Indianola.  Reported by the Lowndes County EMA.

The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home in Lawnreceville on Green Oak Circle putting a hole in the roof and setting the home on fire.  The fire was exinguished within 30 minutes.

The Jackson county Deputy Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst or microburst had caused considerable damage in the city of Pendergrass.  The City Hall and an adjacent temporary garage sustained significant damage, as well as a nearby vehicle.  Numerous trees and several power lines were also blown down in the area and power outages were widespread throughout the city for several hours.  In Jefferson, the public reported that the roof was blown off a Wendy's restaurant and several trees were blown down as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray county 911 center, the public, and the Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that a number of trees were blown down across the southern portion of the county, especially from Holley to Ramhurst and southward to the Gordon county line.  The greatest damage appeared to be in the area between Ramhurst and Carters Lake, along old U.S. Highway 411 and Georgia Highway 136, where a number of large trees, some greater than two feet in diameter, were blown down onto the highways and several surrounding roads.  County workers spent seven or more hours clearing the fallen trees from the roads.  CSX railroad tracks in the area were also blocked for hours because of downed trees.  Two women in the area were injured when a tree fell on them.  Several large trees were also reported to have been blown down in the Holley area.  Several thousand people in the county were left without power for several hours during the event.

The Murray county 911 center reported golf ball-sized hail in Holley and the public reported penny-sized hail in Holley.  The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that golf ball-sized hail covered the porch at one residence in the area.
The public reported that penny-sized hail fell for 30 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga county 911 center reported extensive flooding of roads in Trion.  Water had risen up to the porches of homes along U. S. Highway 27 in areas near Spring Branch and Cane Creeks.  Several streets in downtown were completely flooded and closed, including 1'st Street, Thomas Road, and Mountain View Road.  Rescue crews were placed on standby.  Radar estimated rainfall totals of around 5 inches in a 2-hour period in this area.  In Summerville, rainfall was somewhat less, but amounts of 2-3 inches were reported by the Summerville Waste Water Treatment Plant.  Several roads in this area were also flooded and impassable.  The Chattooga river quickly rose above flood stage.
The Washington county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down.
The Houston county 911 center reported that a couple of trees were blown down.
The Macon county 911 center reported that a couple of trees were blown down.
The Sumter Electric Membership Cooperative reported that one tree and one power line had been blown down.

The Dalton Fire and Rescue Department, along with the county 911 center and the public reported that extensive and major flooding was occurring in a relatively small 10 mile by 10 mile wide area just north of Dalton.  Rainfall in excess of five inches occurred between 7 pm and midnight EDT within this area.  Several roads were completely flooded, rendered impassable, and closed.  In addition, several people became stranded in the area and required rescues.
The Murray county 911 center reported that heavy rain caused ponding of water on the roadways and in low lying areas, but no flash flooding or traffic problems were reported
The Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that lightning strikes knocked out an electric company switch and broke a couple of utility poles.  Several power outages in the area resulted.

The Whitfield county 911 center reported that a number of trees and power lines were blown down across the county.  The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that one tree fell on a home in Dalton and another fell on an abandoned car.  In another incident, a girl was injured when a tree fell on her as she was coming in from docking a boat.  Several thousand people in the county were left without power for several hours during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilkes county Emergency Management Director reported that a number of trees had been blown down across the county.  Widespread power outages were observed as well.
The public reported penny to quarter-sized hail.  One large 30-40 foot Birch tree was also split in two in Newnan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Morgan county Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail.
The Morgan county Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were blown down.
The Taylor County News of Butler reported that lightning struck an empty house being used for storage and set it on fire.  The house and its contents were a total loss.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The Paulding county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded and impassable, especially in the Calhoun area.
Dime to quarter size hail reported by the Worth County Sheriff.

The National Weather Service cooperative observer, located in Sautee in White county, reported that 5.38 inches of rain had fallen since 7 am EDT.  Widespread flooding of low-lying areas around the county, including some roads, was observed as a result.
The public reported that drainage ditches in the area were well out of their banks and water was flowing across the roads.

A storm spotter reported extensive flooding of roads in the Rutledge area.  Water, six inches or greater in depth, was flowing across roads in the area as well as across the yards of several homes.  In Bostwick, a number of roads were flooded and impassable with water flowing across the roads.
The Social Circle Fire Department reported that a few roads in the area were flooded and impassable with water flowing across the roads.
The public reported that water was flowing over Georgia Highway 53.
The public reported penny to quarter-sized hail in Mableton and Smyrna, with nickel-sized hail west of Smyrna.
A few downed trees and power lines reported by the Lowndes County EMA.

Numerous downed trees and power lines in the towns of Attapulgus and Climax.  A roof was blown off a mobile home along Highway 27 about four miles north of Attapulgus.  Reported by Decatur County 911.
The Spalding county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down on Georgia Highway 155.

The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that a 27-year old male was struck by lightning while mowing a lawn.   The thunderstorm was not directly over the location at the time, but was nearby in Lawrenceville.   The young man was pronounced dead on arrival at a local medical center.
The Banks county Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees were down in Homer and some power lines were down near Maysville on the Jackson county line.
Several downed trees across State Road 133 reported by the Dougherty County Sheriff.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that extensive flooding of roads occurred across the county.  The worst situation was in Canton, where in some cases, several feet of water was flowing over the roads.  Nearly three feet of water was reported on Georgia Highway 5 at Interstate-575.  Nearby on Riverton Parkway at Interstate-575, several vehicles became trapped in three to four foot deep flood waters covering the roads.  Thus, several people had to be rescued from their vehicles.  In the Salacoa Community in the far northwest part of the county, flash flooding damaged a drainage pipe and roadway, while in the Hickory Flat community, about six miles southeast of Canton, a small creek rose over 14 feet out of its banks destroying part of Epperson Road and an associated concrete drainage structure.  Local storm spotters reported 3 inches of rain in this area within 30 minutes.

The public reported that three trees were blown down near Whitestone in extreme southwest Gilmer county.
The public reported penny to half-dollar-sized hail near Whitestone, near the Pickens county border, in extreme southwest Gilmer county.
The Catoosa county 911 center reported that several more trees and power lines were blown down in the area.
The Catoosa county 911 center reported that several roads across the south portion of the county were flooded and closed.

The Emanuel county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Madison county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down in Pocataligo.
The Carroll county Emergency Management Director reported that a few trees were blown down from Temple, to Mount Zion, to Bowdon.
The Polk county 911 center reported that lightning set a structure on fire.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Bill, which was earlier Tropical Storm Bill, tracked across north and central Georgia during the day bringing heavy rain, flooding, wind damage, and even an isolated tornado to the region.  The storm, which formed in the Gulf of Mexico Sunday morning, June 29th, moved inland between New Orleans, Louisiana and Mobile, Alabama on Monday June 30th, then tracked northeast to near Tuscaloosa, Alabama by the morning of July 30th, then turned east-northeast and accelerated.  The depression moved between Birmingham, Alabama and Atlanta, Georgia during the afternoon of July 1st, exiting northeast Georgia after midnight on July 2nd.  Twenty-four rainfall totals of four to six inches were common on July 1st across much of north and portions of central Georgia, roughly north of a line from Columbus to Athens.  Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range south of this line.  There were numerous reports of flooding, especially in the Atlanta metropolitan area, and a number of roads were rendered impassable and closed.  The ground across north and central Georgia was saturated from a number of weeks of above normal rainfall and the tropical storm rainfall just exacerbated the situation.  As the center of circulation associated with the tropical depression tracked across north Georgia, a brief  F1 tornado spinup occurred in Morgan county southwest of Madison in east central Georgia.  There were also other isolated wind damage reports in areas east and southeast of Atlanta from Stockbridge to Madison to Athens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.
A television station in Macon received a report from the public that five to six trees had been blown down.
The Hall county 911 center reported that at least three, possibly four trees were down in the city.  One of the trees was down on a power line.
Highway dept reported trees down.
The Gilmer county 911 center reported that heavy thunderstorm rains resulted in flooding of one road in the city with water flowing across the road.  A business parking lot was also flooded out.
The Warren county Emergency Management Director reported that a shed was blown over at a gasoline service station.
The Norwood Post Office reported dime-sized hail.
Two to four inches of rain caused flooding on Highways 27 and 39.  Reported by the Early County EMA.
TV meteorologist reported large limbs and power lines down in south Augusta.
Highway dept reported trees down.

The Gilmer county Emergency Management Director reported that strong straight line winds or a microburst/downburst had resulted in widespread damage to trees along U.S. Highway 76/Georgia Highway 282 west of Ellijay between Elders and Roosevelt.  The damage path was up to four miles long and one mile wide.  Numerous trees were snapped off approximately 10 to 12 feet above the ground in this area.  Most of the damage was between Cross Roads Church Road and Pleasant Grove Church Road.  The worst of the damage was near Dover Chappel Road.  No damage to structures was reported.
The Gilmer county Emergency Management Director reported that excessive rainfall had resulted in some minor street flooding along U.S. Highway 76/Georgia Highway 282.
The public reported that heavy rain had resulted in minor flooding of some streets and particularly underpasses.
The Bartow county 911 center reported that a couple of trees had been blown down, one on Georgia Highway 293 just east of Kingston and the other on Macedonia Road between Kingston and Taylorsville.
Three to five inches of rain flooded several roads including Highways 129 and 168 in Nashville.  Reported by the Berrien County EMA.

The Coweta county 911 center reported that a couple of trees and a power line were down at Fisher Road and Wynn's Pond Road near the Fayette county border.  An off-duty National Weather Service employee reported that a couple of large tree limbs were down on Lower Fayetteville Road south of Sharpsburg.
The public reported nickel-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic cold front sent temperatures to their lowest level in several years across north and central Georgia. As the cold front moved through temperatures dropped through the 20s during the day and into the teens by evening accompanied with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts.  By midnight several locations were already below 10 degrees.  Minimum temperatures bottomed out in the single digits over nearly all of north Georgia Friday morning, January 24th, with lows mostly in the mid teens in central Georgia.  Hiawassee in Towns county recorded the statewide low with -2 degrees F.  Some locations in north Georgia failed to rise above the freezing mark for slightly over two days.  Several county school districts across the north part of the state were closed on the 23rd and/or 24th because of the extreme cold

An extended period of below normal temperatures was observed as a Polar vortex in the northeastern United States provided a cold northwest flow to the region.  A series of Canadian/Arctic air masses invaded the region during the period.  Temperatures averaged 10 to 20 degrees below normal during the period, with a hard freeze recorded at most location nearly every day between the 11th and the 19th.  On the 18th, a strong Arctic high pressure system caused minimum temperatures to drop into the teens in most areas...with some single digit temperatures in the northeast mountains.

A large Polar vortex, anchored over the Hudson Bay region of Canada and the northeastern United States, dominated the eastern United States nearly the entire month.  As a result, a cold, dry northwest flow prevailed into the southeastern United States throughout the month.  Gulf moisture was virtually shut off from weather systems as disturbances moved down into the area from the Northern Plains and Ohio Valley.  This pattern resulted in very little precipitation during the first 28 days of the month.  During the last three days of the month, a stronger southern jet stream brought rain back into the area.  Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

As the cold front moved through temperatures dropped through the 20s during the day and into the teens by evening accompanied with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts.  By midnight several locations were already below 10 degrees.  Minimum temperatures bottomed out in the single digits over nearly all of north Georgia Friday morning, January 24th, with lows mostly in the mid teens in central Georgia.  Hiawassee in Towns county recorded the statewide low with -2 degrees F.  Some locations in north Georgia failed to rise above the freezing mark for slightly over two days.  Several county school districts across the north part of the state were closed on the 23rd and/or 24th because of the extreme cold

Light snow, mixed with some light pockets of sleet, fell across north central and northeast Georgia.  The snow was the result of a strong arctic cold front moving through the region as a weak upper-level disturbance moved over the area.  Snowfall was confined to areas generally northeast of a line from Lafayette, to Canton, to Cumming, to Gainesville.  The heaviest snow fell in the northeast Georgia mountain counties of Fannin, Towns, Union, and White, where snowfall amounts up to 3 inches were common.  Elsewhere, snowfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  Many of the roads, especially at the higher elevations of the northeast mountains, became icy and hazardous. Some of the mountain roads in Union and White counties became impassable and had to be closed.  Numerous accidents were also reported in these counties, as well as in Hall county where some sleet mixed with the snow. Strong, gusty 20 to 30 mph north winds caused blowing of the snow, especially in the areas that received more than one inch.  The snow was followed by a period of extreme cold with temperatures in the teens.

A large Polar vortex, anchored over the Hudson Bay region of Canada and the northeastern United States, dominated the eastern United States nearly the entire month.  As a result, a cold, dry northwest flow prevailed into the southeastern United States throughout the month.  Gulf moisture was virtually shut off from weather systems as disturbances moved down into the area from the Northern Plains and Ohio Valley.  This pattern resulted in very little precipitation during the first 28 days of the month.  During the last three days of the month, a stronger southern jet stream brought rain back into the area.  Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

Light snow fell across much of far north Georgia as a strong Arctic cold front moved through the region. Most of the snow fell north of a line from Buchanan, to Atlanta, to Cumming, to Homer.  Snowfall amounts averaged from one-half to one inch in most of this area, with up to 2 inches common in the northeast Georgia mountain counties of  Fannin, Union, and Towns.  Cherokee and Murray county also reported amounts in excess of one inch.  The snow combined with 20 to 30 mph and gusty north winds and temperatures in the teens and twenties to create slick and hazardous roads.   However, no roads were reported to have been closed.  Nonetheless, numerous accidents were reported, especially in Cherokee, Bartow, and Forsyth counties, several with minor injuries.  Thirty-eight accidents were reported in Forsyth county alone, with 29 accidents in Bartow.  The amount of accidents in Cherokee county was unspecified.  Schools were closed in all of these counties and several surrounding counties because of the snow and extreme cold.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of an intense microburst and a tornado produced winds estimated at 120-130mph.  A barn and camper trailer.  Moderate damage was done to 4 homes with minor damage to 34 other homes.  Debris from the barn was found a mile downstream.  The microburst width was 1/8-1/4 mile wide with the vortex circulation about 50 yds wide.
Sheriff reported scattered trees down from Harlem to Appling.

An extended period of below normal temperatures was observed as a Polar vortex in the northeastern United States provided a cold northwest flow to the region.  A series of Canadian/Arctic air masses invaded the region during the period.  Temperatures averaged 10 to 20 degrees below normal during the period, with a hard freeze recorded at most location nearly every day between the 11th and the 19th.  On the 18th, a strong Arctic high pressure system caused minimum temperatures to drop into the teens in most areas...with some single digit temperatures in the northeast mountains.

A weak upper-level disturbance combined with temperatures near 32 degrees Fahrenheit to produce light snow across several north Georgia counties. While most of these counties only received a trace of snow or sleet, the higher elevations of Dade, Dawson, Towns, Union, and White counties received 1 to 2 inches of snow.

An extended period of below normal temperatures was observed as a Polar vortex in the northeastern United States provided a cold northwest flow to the region.  A series of Canadian/Arctic air masses invaded the region during the period.  Temperatures averaged 10 to 20 degrees below normal during the period, with a hard freeze recorded at most location nearly every day between the 11th and the 19th.  On the 18th, a strong Arctic high pressure system caused minimum temperatures to drop into the teens in most areas...with some single digit temperatures in the northeast mountains.

A strong Arctic cold front sent temperatures to their lowest level in several years across north and central Georgia. As the cold front moved through temperatures dropped through the 20s during the day and into the teens by evening accompanied with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts.  By midnight several locations were already below 10 degrees.  Minimum temperatures bottomed out in the single digits over nearly all of north Georgia Friday morning, January 24th, with lows mostly in the mid teens in central Georgia.  Hiawassee in Towns county recorded the statewide low with -2 degrees F.  Some locations in north Georgia failed to rise above the freezing mark for slightly over two days.  Several county school districts across the north part of the state were closed on the 23rd and/or 24th because of the extreme cold

Light snow, mixed with some light pockets of sleet, fell across north central and northeast Georgia.  The snow was the result of a strong arctic cold front moving through the region as a weak upper-level disturbance moved over the area.  Snowfall was confined to areas generally northeast of a line from Lafayette, to Canton, to Cumming, to Gainesville.  The heaviest snow fell in the northeast Georgia mountain counties of Fannin, Towns, Union, and White, where snowfall amounts up to 3 inches were common.  Elsewhere, snowfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  Many of the roads, especially at the higher elevations of the northeast mountains, became icy and hazardous. Some of the mountain roads in Union and White counties became impassable and had to be closed.  Numerous accidents were also reported in these counties, as well as in Hall county where some sleet mixed with the snow. Strong, gusty 20 to 30 mph north winds caused blowing of the snow, especially in the areas that received more than one inch.  The snow was followed by a period of extreme cold with temperatures in the teens.

The Rome News Tribune reported that strong post-cold frontal winds gusting from 40 to 45 mph blew a 40-foot section of roof off the top of a downtown Rome furniture store.  In addition, two trees were blown down, one on Dalton Road and another on Horseleg Creek Road.  Winds in the 30 to 35 mph range, associated with this same cold front, also caused damage further south later in the afternoon.  An off duty National Weather Service employee reported that a large 50 foot tall or greater pine tree was broken in two by strong winds.   The tree landed on and caused damage to a fence.

A large Polar vortex, anchored over the Hudson Bay region of Canada and the northeastern United States, dominated the eastern United States nearly the entire month.  As a result, a cold, dry northwest flow prevailed into the southeastern United States throughout the month.  Gulf moisture was virtually shut off from weather systems as disturbances moved down into the area from the Northern Plains and Ohio Valley.  This pattern resulted in very little precipitation during the first 28 days of the month.  During the last three days of the month, a stronger southern jet stream brought rain back into the area.  Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

Light snow fell across much of far north Georgia as a strong Arctic cold front moved through the region. Most of the snow fell north of a line from Buchanan, to Atlanta, to Cumming, to Homer.  Snowfall amounts averaged from one-half to one inch in most of this area, with up to 2 inches common in the northeast Georgia mountain counties of  Fannin, Union, and Towns.  Cherokee and Murray county also reported amounts in excess of one inch.  The snow combined with 20 to 30 mph and gusty north winds and temperatures in the teens and twenties to create slick and hazardous roads.   However, no roads were reported to have been closed.  Nonetheless, numerous accidents were reported, especially in Cherokee, Bartow, and Forsyth counties, several with minor injuries.  Thirty-eight accidents were reported in Forsyth county alone, with 29 accidents in Bartow.  The amount of accidents in Cherokee county was unspecified.  Schools were closed in all of these counties and several surrounding counties because of the snow and extreme cold.

Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

An extended period of below normal temperatures was observed as a Polar vortex in the northeastern United States provided a cold northwest flow to the region.  A series of Canadian/Arctic air masses invaded the region during the period.  Temperatures averaged 10 to 20 degrees below normal during the period, with a hard freeze recorded at most location nearly every day between the 11th and the 19th.  On the 18th, a strong Arctic high pressure system caused minimum temperatures to drop into the teens in most areas...with some single digit temperatures in the northeast mountains.

Light snow fell across much of far north Georgia as a strong Arctic cold front moved through the region. Most of the snow fell north of a line from Buchanan, to Atlanta, to Cumming, to Homer.  Snowfall amounts averaged from one-half to one inch in most of this area, with up to 2 inches common in the northeast Georgia mountain counties of  Fannin, Union, and Towns.  Cherokee and Murray county also reported amounts in excess of one inch.  The snow combined with 20 to 30 mph and gusty north winds and temperatures in the teens and twenties to create slick and hazardous roads.   However, no roads were reported to have been closed.  Nonetheless, numerous accidents were reported, especially in Cherokee, Bartow, and Forsyth counties, several with minor injuries.  Thirty-eight accidents were reported in Forsyth county alone, with 29 accidents in Bartow.  The amount of accidents in Cherokee county was unspecified.  Schools were closed in all of these counties and several surrounding counties because of the snow and extreme cold.

A strong Arctic cold front sent temperatures to their lowest level in several years across north and central Georgia. As the cold front moved through temperatures dropped through the 20s during the day and into the teens by evening accompanied with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts.  By midnight several locations were already below 10 degrees.  Minimum temperatures bottomed out in the single digits over nearly all of north Georgia Friday morning, January 24th, with lows mostly in the mid teens in central Georgia.  Hiawassee in Towns county recorded the statewide low with -2 degrees F.  Some locations in north Georgia failed to rise above the freezing mark for slightly over two days.  Several county school districts across the north part of the state were closed on the 23rd and/or 24th because of the extreme cold

A weak upper-level disturbance combined with temperatures near 32 degrees Fahrenheit to produce light snow across several north Georgia counties. While most of these counties only received a trace of snow or sleet, the higher elevations of Dade, Dawson, Towns, Union, and White counties received 1 to 2 inches of snow.

A large Polar vortex, anchored over the Hudson Bay region of Canada and the northeastern United States, dominated the eastern United States nearly the entire month.  As a result, a cold, dry northwest flow prevailed into the southeastern United States throughout the month.  Gulf moisture was virtually shut off from weather systems as disturbances moved down into the area from the Northern Plains and Ohio Valley.  This pattern resulted in very little precipitation during the first 28 days of the month.  During the last three days of the month, a stronger southern jet stream brought rain back into the area.  Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

Many locations in north and central Georgia were having their driest January in history prior to the 29th, when 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain fell across much of the area.  For the first 28 days of the month, many areas had not even received 0.50 inch of rain or liquid equivalent of snow.  Nonetheless, the lack of rain during the first 28 days left most areas with a substantial rainfall deficit for the month, including Macon with a deficit of 3.55 inches, Atlanta with a deficit of 3.03 inches, Athens with a deficit of 2.95 inches, and Columbus with a deficit of 2.66 inches.  January is normally a rainy month for north and central Georgia with normal rainfall amounts in the 4.00 to 5.00 inch range.

An extended period of below normal temperatures was observed as a Polar vortex in the northeastern United States provided a cold northwest flow to the region.  A series of Canadian/Arctic air masses invaded the region during the period.  Temperatures averaged 10 to 20 degrees below normal during the period, with a hard freeze recorded at most location nearly every day between the 11th and the 19th.  On the 18th, a strong Arctic high pressure system caused minimum temperatures to drop into the teens in most areas...with some single digit temperatures in the northeast mountains.

A strong Arctic cold front sent temperatures to their lowest level in several years across north and central Georgia. As the cold front moved through temperatures dropped through the 20s during the day and into the teens by evening accompanied with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts.  By midnight several locations were already below 10 degrees.  Minimum temperatures bottomed out in the single digits over nearly all of north Georgia Friday morning, January 24th, with lows mostly in the mid teens in central Georgia.  Hiawassee in Towns county recorded the statewide low with -2 degrees F.  Some locations in north Georgia failed to rise above the freezing mark for slightly over two days.  Several county school districts across the north part of the state were closed on the 23rd and/or 24th because of the extreme cold
