The Cobb county Emergency Management Director reported that extensive flooding of roads was occurring across much of the county.  The flooding forced the evacuation of several residents in various areas of the county.  The National Weather Service river gage at Marietta on Sope Creek showed that very heavy rainfall had forced the creek quickly into a flash flood stage.  The creek crested at 13.5 feet around 8 pm EDT, well above the 12.0 foot flood stage.  Minor flooding of low-lying areas, including property and roads in the immediate vicinity, resulted.  The Marietta Daily Journal reported that only one acre of a 22-acre farm in Austell was spared flooding.  Butler Creek overflowed its banks along Nance Road in Acworth resulting in nearly half-a-foot of water flowing over the Nance Road Bridge.  Some homes were also flooded as well.
An amateur radio operator reported that some power lines were down in Columbus.
The Macon county Sheriff's Office reported that a number of trees were blown down across the county.
The public reported that  some trees were blown down as well as some highway signs that were blown onto the highway.
The Harris county Emergency Manager reported that a number of trees were blown down throughout the county.

The Troup county 911and the La Grange newspaper reported that a number of trees were blown down around the county, especially on the west side of La Grange.  At least five homes and one vehicle were damaged when large trees fell on them. A brick fence and an iron and brick fence at another location were also damaged.  One man received injuries as ceiling tiles fell on him when the ceiling in his living room collapsed during the strong thunderstorm winds.  Another family of five was trapped inside of their home when a large tree fell on the house.  However, no injuries were reported.  The Troup county 911 center received reports of fallen trees at 40 different locations and at least six roads in the county were blocked by downed trees.  County workers spent most of the night removing debris from blocked roadways.
The public and an amateur radio operator reported penny-sized hail.
Large tree limbs down.
Numerous trees and power lines down.
Several trees were down.
Numerous power lines and trees were down.

The public reported a 15-mile long path of damage across northeast Bleckley county, beginning near Cary and terminating near Rebie, along which numerous trees were blown down, a 20-foot high empty water tank was blown over, a barn was destroyed, and  a house suffered minor damage.  While much of the damage was caused by strong straight-line thunderstorm winds, a survey conducted by the National Weather Service Forecast Office in Peachtree City, concluded that a severe microburst or gustnado, moving in a southeastward direction, affected a small area approximately 60 yards wide and 200 yards long beginning approximately two miles northwest of Goldsboro along Georgia Highway 278,.  Numerous trees were blown down along Georgia Highway 278, all felled to the north, while other trees, blown down approximately one mile south of this location, were felled to the south.  Residents reported seeing a funnel cloud in the Cary area.
Numerous large tree limbs down.
Power lines downed.
Several large trees down across the western side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees and power lines down county-wide.
Law enforcement reported a large tree was uprooted and fell onto a trailer.
Law enforcement officials reported two trees and numerous power lines down in Pearson.
911 dispatch reported numerous trees and power lines down.
Law enforcement reported trees down just east of Millwood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved from Madison County into northwest Elbert County and intensified, destroying or severely damaging several houses and mobile homes. A parked car was also flipped before the tornado lifted.
Entire row of trees blown down.
Large tree down on home one mile west of Interstate 95.  Large tree down on horse stable one and a half miles west of Interstate 95.
Numerous large trees and power lines down across southern portion of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waycross 911 dispatch reported numerous trees and power lines down along Dewey Road, Nichols Highway and Pancake Rd.
Law enforcement officials reported numerous trees and power lines down county-wide.
Law enforcement officials reported numerous trees and power lines down county-wide.
Law enforcement reported trees and power lines were down in Homerville. Dime size hail was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down near Brays Lake and damaged several homes and businesses along its 6-mile track.
Trees were blown down at Pullian Lane and Liberty Hill Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Jesup police reported several trees down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: EOC reported trees down.
EOC reported 3/4 inch hail on hwy 305.
SKYWARN spotter reported large branches down on Tobacco road in Augusta.
EOC reported trees and powerlines down near Scotts Corner.
SKYWARN spotter reported high water covering one area of US 1 and Bobby Jones Highway.
EOC reported trees down and a shed destroyed at a home on hwy 25.
Hail was reported near Tallulah Falls.
Keysville FD reported 3/4 inch hail and 1.5 inches of rain.
The Taylor county 911 center reported that a few power lines were down.
Sheriff reported trees down on Brown road.

The Troup county 911 center reported a number of roads in the La Grange area were flooded as heavy rain and thunderstorms once again moved over the area.  While flooding was the worst in La Grange, some flooding was also reported in other parts of the county as well.
The Cordele county 911 center reported that two trees were blown down.

The Johnson Journal of Wrightsville reported that very heavy rain, up to four inches during the evening hours, caused the awning on a one-year old building to fall on two male patrons that were standing underneath.  One of the men was seriously injured and remained in critical condition several hours later.  The other also sustained significant injuries.
The Rome News Tribune reported that several trees fell during the combination of strong thunderstorm winds and torrential rainfall.  One tree fell on a power line.
The Taylor County News of Butler reported that very heavy rain on top of very heavy rain less than 24-hours before resulted in flooding of a number of roads and highways in the county.
The Lumpkin county 911 center reported that flash flooding had occurred in a small area northwest of Dahlonega, resulting in damage to Horton Road.

The Gwinnett county SkyWarn reported that a home in Dacula was struck by lightning.  The fire department investigated, but no fire was reported. Damage was confined to electronic equipment.   Lightning also was responsible for a sewage leak at a pump station near Snellville as the pump station was briefly rendered inoperable by the lightning strike.

The Fayette county 911 center and Emergency Management Director reported that extensive flooding of roads and homes was occurring just west and just north of downtown Fayetteville.  At Georgia Highway 85 and Georgia Highway 314, just north of Fayetteville, the road had two feet of water over it.  Just west of Fayetteville off Georgia Highway 54 near Gingercake Creek, water rose several feet up on homes causing extensive damage to a number of homes.  One home had a portion of a lower wall cave in from the weight and rush of the flood waters.  The dam on Pye Lake, also just west of Fayetteville, was also breached from the flood waters.  Consequently, several homes in the LaFayette Gardens Home subdivision were flooded and several residents in the Cornwallis and Wetherly Drive area of this subdivision had to be evacuated.  At least five vehicles were damaged or destroyed by the flood waters as driveways collapsed and the vehicles fell into or were submerged by the flood waters.  Several roads in downtown Fayetteville and nearby adjacent areas, especially to the west and north, sustained significant damage, Portions of the roads were washed out and extensive repairs were required.

The Clayton county 911 center reported that flash flooding had occurred in the Briargate Subdivision as a result of Briar Creek flooding the local area.  Up to eight homes had to be evacuated on Winding Way in the subdivision.

The Douglas county Emergency Management Director reported that extensive flooding occurred across the northern portions of the county, especially in the Lithia Springs, Douglasville, and Winston areas.  Several homes were flooded and at least eight roads sustained damage, including some with washed out bridges and culverts.  The Douglas County Sentinel reported that the Prestley Mill and Slater Mill Road bridges suffered the most damage.  Extensive dirt and debris had to be removed from the roadways after the water receded before they could even be used.  In addition, the guard rails on the bridge were bent from the force of the rushing water.  An off duty National Weather Service employee reported that nearly five inches of rain fell across northern Douglas county between midnight and 5 am EDT.
The Forsyth county 911 center reported that some trees and power lines were down near the intersection of Georgia Highway 9 and Georgia Highway 306 in the Pirkle Woods community.

The Summerville News reported that a large number of trees had been blown down across the county by thunderstorm winds, although the majority of the monetary damage was noted in Summerville.  The downed trees caused damage to three homes.  One home in Summerville suffered extensive damage when a tree fell through the roof.  A number of power lines were also brought down as the trees fell.  A street sign was blown from a pole in downtown Summerville and a window was blown out a local attorney's office in downtown.  The public also reported pea-sized hail during the event.
The Hall county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that a few trees were down.
The Heard county 911 center reported that there were several areas around the county of minor street flooding from persistent heavy rain.

The local newspaper for Jonesboro reported that lightning struck the Clayton County sewage transfer plant resulting in the spillage of over 150,000 gallons of treated sewage into the Flint River.  This occurred when the lightning rendered the pump at the station inoperable.  The spill was not known because the automatic system, which notifies the plant of such failures, was also rendered inoperative by the lightning strike.
The Calhoun police department reported penny-sized hail.

Fox 5 News of Atlanta reported that extensive flooding was occurring across the eastern portion of Cobb county.  Nearly two feet of water was flowing over Paper Mill Road at Sope Creek.  Johnson Ferry Road near Connemara Drive and Dartmoor Drive was completely underwater. Residents of the West Chase Apartment Homes and Oakhill Town Home Association were stranded when flood waters caused a 20-feet deep and 6-feet wide sink hole causing the closure of Six Flags Parkway.  Shallowford and Lassiter Drive in east Cobb county were also flooded.  Several roads were washed out and had to be closed.

Fox 5 News of Atlanta reported that extensive flooding was occurring in the Brookhaven area.  Several hundred people in Brookhaven had to be rescued from their apartments.   This flooding was attributed to the rapid rise of Peachtree Creek.  In some places the water was 10 feet deep.  Several other roads across the northern portion of the county were also flooded, including Clairmont Road and Buford Highway.  One resident on Drew Valley Road in Decatur reported that her neighbor's play set washed into her yard over an 8 foot fence.
The White county 911 center reported that two males, one a 9-year old and the other a 44-year old, were injured when struck by lightning.  The injuries were reported to be minor.

The Gwinnett county 911 center, as well as the county Skywarn, and local newspapers reported extensive flooding of roads and streets across much of the county.  Flash flooding essentially began in the Dacula area as a thunderstorm dumped over one inch of rain per hour between 11 pm EDT Monday the 16th and 130 am EDT Tuesday the 17th.  Rainfall of three to five inches was common between midnight EDT and 8 am EDT the 17th across much of the county.  By 1251 am EDT, at least 3 inches of water was flowing over Rutherford Road and Superior Road in Dacula, but the road was still passable.  By 1:20 am EDT, however, significant flooding was reported on many streets in Dacula as the very heavy rain continued.  By 133 am EDT, the intersection of Springdale Road and Georgia Highway 124 in Snellville was four feet under water in Snellville.  By 149 am EDT, U.S. Highway 29, Winder Highway, had two feet of water flowing over it and was going to be closed by local law enforcement authorities.  Later a 48-inch water line at this location was washed away.  A bridge on Hillcrest Road was three feet under water during the height of the storm.  In Lilburn, residents of several homes in the Connemara subdivision area at Hillcrest Road and Indian Trail, near Interstate-85, had to be evacuated as flood waters from the Yellow River and Sweetwater Creek stranded them in their neighborhood and threatened their homes.  One home in the area reported nearly 2 feet of water flowing through their backyard.  A sink hole was reported to have developed on Fence Road in this area.  Extensive flooding of roads was also reported in Duluth during the late night and early morning hours.  Several homes, about one-quarter mile northeast of U.S. Highway 29 and Sugarloaf Parkway in Lawrenceville, were flooded.  All together, 13 roads in the county had to be closed because of flooding.  Other structures also suffered damage from the flood waters, including a greenhouse business on Georgia Highway 124, with an estimated loss of $7,000 dollars, and the historic Alcovy River Gristmill dam, on which the upper three feet of the dam were lost from the force of the flood waters.  A sewage pump station near U.S. Highway 78 in Snellville was also rendered inoperable for a brief period by flood waters.  One vehicle was destroyed and another suffered major damage from the flood waters.  A female resident of Dacula had to be rescued from a 2000 Silverado pickup truck when the truck was submerged in flood waters on Brooks Road off Bramlett Shoals Road.  Shortly after the rescue, the vehicle was swept away by the flood waters.  A 1999 BMW was also damaged when flood waters rose above the tires and water damaged the interior of the car.

The Johnson county 911 center, as well as the public, reported that a small dam failed and washed out a road.  The Johnson Journal of Wrightsville reported that up to four inches of rain fell in parts of the county during the evening.
The Gwinnett county 911 center reported that there were several reports of trees down in various areas throughout the county.
Local Atlanta broadcast radio reported that several roads in the northern portion of the county were flooded and impassable.
The Jackson county 911 center reported that several creeks in the area quickly rose out of their banks rendering several roads flooded and impassable.
The Forsyth county 911 center reported that several roads in the central portion of the county were flooded and impassable.

The Carroll county Emergency Management Director reported that an earthen dam on Lake Dakota near Whitesburg collapsed causing the flooding and closure of Ramsdell Road.  Heavy rain flooded a number of roads and bridges across the county and forced the closing of 20 roads around the county.  Some of the roads would require a week or more to repair.  The Carroll Star News reported that Molette Street in Carrollton was completely washed out.  A home in Carrollton was also flooded.

The Chattooga county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the county were flooded, several of which had to be closed.  Some businesses in downtown Summerville also sustained damage from flood waters.
The Floyd county 911 center reported that a number of roads were flooded across the northern portion of the county.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that several roads were flooded and closed.  Several creeks around the county were also flowing out of their banks.
The public reported that a number of tree limbs had been blown down or broken off trees by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large oaks were down in Osprey Cove and Cardinal Place East. Piles of pine trees were noted down across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fox 5 News of Atlanta reported that extensive flooding was occurring along Northridge Crossing Drive in the area between Sandy Springs and Dunwoody near the De Kalb county border.  Several town homes had to be evacuated as water rose up to the level of mailboxes.  A number of roads in the north and central portion of the county were flooded with water flowing across the roads.  Flooding on Roswell Road resulted in a large sinkhole at one point in the road and washed out a portion of the road in a another location.  Peachtree Creek quickly rose to flood stage of 17 feet in a 3 hour period, resulting in the usual flooding of roads, homes, businesses, and other property near the creek.

The Daily Citizen Newspaper of Dalton and the Chatsworth Times, reported that Murray county suffered extensive flooding from excessive rain.  Particularly hard hit was Chatsworth, where flash floods swamped downtown neighborhoods and businesses with water.  Dozens of roads were flooded and several, especially in downtown Chatsworth, were closed.  Tibbs Bridge Road, leading west to Whitfield county, was over a foot under water.  In some downtown areas, the water was 4-feet deep.  Several businesses were literally washed out.  At one store, flood waters washed nearly half of the store's merchandise into the parking lot.  The Murray Plaza Shopping Center was also flooded and the Spring Lakes Golf Course was flooded when an adjacent stream quickly rose out of its banks.  Much of the area surrounding the stream was also submerged in water when the creek overflowed its banks.  At least two homes were evacuated because of flood waters and one home on Cohutta Street was flooded for the fourth time in less than a year!  The 4-feet deep flood waters on Cohutta Street also prevented county firefighters from reaching a heart attack patient in a timely fashion.

Trained SkyWarn spotters reported torrential rainfall on Interstate 285 at Georgia Highway 400 resulting in over 2 inches of standing water on the roadway.  Traffic was brought to a standstill at the time in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manger in Wayne County reported 3-4 inches of rainfall in less than 1 hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Polk county 911 center reported that two trees were blown down.  It was largely believed that the trees were weakened because of persistent heavy rain in the area at the time.
The Chattahoochee county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down.

The Bibb county Emergency Management Director reported that extensive flooding had occurred in parts of Macon.  Several streets, roads, and railroad crossings were flooded with up to 1.5 feet of water over roads and into buildings in some areas.  Riverside Drive was closed because of flooding.  The most extensive flooding reported was to a nursing home.  Over one-and-a-half feet of water got into the nursing home causing significant inconvenience and damage.
The Crisp county 911 center reported that one tree was blown down.
The public reported that lightning struck a tree adjacent to a home, setting a branch on fire.  No fire to the home was reported, however.

The Douglas county 911 center reported that heavy rain and thunderstorms renewed flooding problems across the county.  A number of roads were flooded and closed.  A dam failed and washed out a bridge in Chapel Hill, just south of Douglasville.  Many of these same roads had been affected by flooding less than 24 hours prior.  Twenty-four to 36 hour rainfall totals for much of Douglas county were near 10 inches.
The Catoosa county 911 center reported that heavy rain and thunderstorms were resulting in minor street flooding in low lying and poor drainage areas throughout the county.
The Walker county 911 center reported that heavy rain and thunderstorms were resulting in minor flooding across the north portion of the county, north and northwest of Chickamauga.

Several reports of hail, ranging in size from pennies to quarters, were received from the public and the county 911 center.  The public reported quarter-sized hail in Gainesville and penny-sized hail in Oakwood.  The county 911 center reported nickel-sized hail in Gainesville.   In one instance, along Browns Bridge Road on the west side of Gainesville, the hail was reported to have covered the ground several inches deep.
The Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and a few power lines were down in the Salacoa community.

The Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that a number of trees and power lines were blown down in Sutalee, in several locations six to eight miles southeast of Canton, in Hickory Flat, and just west of Woodstock.

The Taliaferro county 911 center reported that a number of roads were flooded across the county.  Several roads had up to one foot of water flowing across the road.  In addition, several creeks were well out of their banks.
Sheriff reported trees and powerlines down in Lincolnton.
The Polk county 911 center reported that heavy rain had resulted in widespread low-land flooding.
A SkyWarn storm spotter reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported golf ball-sized hail.
The Treutlen county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.

The Dooly county 911 center reported that excessive rainfall of six to eight inches in just a few hours had resulted in the flooding of several roads in the southwest part of the county.  Several streams and creeks had quickly risen out of their banks and water was flowing across several roads.
The Emanuel county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.
The Emanuel county 911 center reported that lightning struck a structure and set it on fire.  Damage estimates were indeterminate.
The Little Ocmulgee Electric Membership Cooperative (EMC) reported that several trees were blown down.
The Bibb county 911 center reported nickel-sized hail.
The Houston county 911 center reported that a couple of trees had been blown down.

The Gordon county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the eastern portion of the county were flooded and impassable.  Rainfall of three to seven inches in a three hour or less period had occurred.  Water was flowing over U.S. 411 at Oakman.  One road in the eastern portion of the county was completely washed out.  One person had to be rescued from flood waters in the eastern portion of the county.  Lick Creek, Redbud Creek, and Dry Creek all quickly rose into flood stage.
The public reported that several roads in the area were flooded with several inches of water flowing over the roads.
The public reported that several roads in the area were flooded with several inches of water flowing over the roads.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The Crisp county 911 center reported that Rossing Crossing Road, between Macadonia Church Road and Coney Road had been washed out by heavy rain.
The Forsyth county Emergency Management Director reported that very heavy rain from persistent thunderstorms caused flooding in the basement of a home.
A SkyWarn spotter for the Gwinnett County ARIES reported a wind gust to 60 mph.

Pond dam burst one quarter mile west of of State Road 268 near Ambrose.  Curtis Decker Rd. rendered impassible by flooding.  Doppler radar estimates of 7 to 10 inches of rain in area over a 6 hour period.

The Fayette county Emergency Management Director reported that a number of trees, and at least four power lines were blown down in the Fayetteville and Peachtree City area.  One very large oak tree fell on a home in Fayetteville causing major damage to the structure.  At least a dozen roads were blocked by fallen trees and the power was out for several hours in the Peachtree City area.  A public works employee was injured later when a tree limb fell out of a tree while he was clearing a roadway.
The public reported penny-sized hail in Stockbridge and quarter-sized hail in Locust Grove.  The hail lasted up to 10 minutes at one location in Locust Grove.
The Times Courier of Ellijay reported that lightning struck a phone line and a power line at the intersection of Old Georgia Highway 5 South and Progress Road causing a small fire.
The Calhoun Times reported that lightning struck a very large tree on Sugar Valley Road.  The tree was blown apart by the lightning strike.

The Times Herald of Newnan reported that lightning struck a home on Jeb Stuart Road setting the attic and roof on fire, both of which suffered major damage.  The master bedroom and bath also suffered minor damage.
The public reported nickel-sized hail.
5 to 6 inches of rainfall flooded creeks and streams.  Dirt secondary roads washed out across western Jeff Davis County.
The Fosyth county Emergency Management Director reported that some trees were blown down.

The Troup county 911 center reported that extensive flooding was occurring in the city.  At least 15 roads were flooded and 30 homes had to be evacuated because of flood waters.  Water got into a number of homes in the city.  Parts of the county reported over 4 inches of rain during the day.
The public reported that very heavy rain was resulting in minor street flooding in the city.
An amateur radio operator measured wind gusts of 70 mph.
The Polk county Fire Department reported that a number of trees and some power lines were down in the Fish Creek area.
The Carroll county 911 center reported that a number of roads across the eastern portion of the county were flooded and closed.
The public reported that Georgia Highway 166 was covered with several inches of flowing water.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

A SkyWarn spotter reported that three to four large trees were blown down on North Cedar Creek Road, three telephone poles were snapped in half on Coney Road just south of McKinney Road and a brick sign was blown down.  The spotter estimated that wind gusts were between 50 and 70 mph.

The Coweta county 911 center reported that there was considerable flooding of roads and bridges across the western and northern portions of the county.  Water was flowing over the bridges on Boy Scout Road,  Georgia Highway 34, and Robert's Road at Caney Creek.  Georgia Highway 34 and Robert's Road were both closed as a result.  In the northern portion of the county, Roscoe Road was flooded and closed at Wahoo Creek.  One vehicle was swept by flood waters into Yellow Jacket Creek.  Rainfall totals in excess of 10 inches were reported from areas north and west of Newnan between Newnan and Roscoe.
The public reported trees down in Conyers and also at the intersection of Georgia Highway 20 and Zingara Road.
The public reported that a house was struck by lightning and set on fire.  Damage estimates were indeterminate.
The public reported that a house was struck by lightning and set on fire.  Damage estimates were indeterminate.
The Walton county 911 center reported nickel-sized hail.
Local news media reported penny-sized hail.
The Putnam county 911 center reported penny-sized hail on U.S. 441 near the Baldwin county line.
The Washington county Emergency Management Director reported nickel-sized hail.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported penny to nickel-sized hail.
The Hancock county Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported that quarter-sized hail fell for up to 10 minutes while a storm spotter reported nickel-sized hail.
The public reported that a few trees were blown down.
The public reported penny-sized hail in Covington and nickel-sized hail in Mansfield.

The public reported hail, ranging in size from dimes to golf balls, which lasted for several minutes.  In one case just east of Bremen, the hail was said to have covered the ground.  It looked as if it had snowed.  Slight hail damage was reported to cars.
An amateur radio operator reported quarter-sized hail.
The Polk county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported nickel to quarter-sized hail.
The Clarke county 911 center reported that a number of trees were down across the northern part of the county.

A tornado touched down near Highway 62, just west of Blakely.  The tornado lifted a mobile home off its foundation, destroyed a barn, damaged several homes and uprooted numerous trees.  Reported by the Early County Deputy Sheriff.

The Bibb county sheriff's office reported that a number of trees were down west of Macon on Columbus Road.  The Lamar County Electric Member Cooperative reported that  a number of power lines were down as a result.
The Crawford county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down along U.S. 80 between Sandy Point and the Bibb county line.

The Columbus Ledger-Enquirer reported that very heavy rain quickly forced the Holland Creek in Columbus to overflow its banks and cause damage to an adjacent sewer line.  Damage was also reported as a result of flooding to the Columbus Convention & Trade Center expansion project, which was well under way.

The Columbus Ledger-Enquirer reported that flooding along the Chattahoochee River caused damage to a floating dock, a number of light poles along the Riverwalk in downtown Columbus.  A number of trees were also uprooted along the river.  The Chattahoochee River crested at 40 feet at noon on May 9th, which is six feet above the floot stage of 34 feet.  This was the highest water level observed since March 17, 1990, when the river crested at 41.4 feet.

The Gilmer county 911 center, along with the Times Courier of Ellijay, reported that excessive rainfall of three to seven inches in a one to three hour period had resulted in extensive flooding, especially along and near Georgia Highway 52 from Ellijay to Cartecay.  Oiltown, Turkey, Clear, and Harris Branch Creeks had all quickly risen well out of their banks flooding out adjacent areas, several roads and bridges, and some homes.  Major flooding was also reported on the Coosawattee River as it flows toward Carters Lake.  Several people in the area had to be rescued from flood waters.  Several roads and bridges in the area were washed out and many had to be closed.  At least one home and a church were washed away by the raging flood waters of the Harris Branch Creek.  The House of Prayer Chapel on Georgia Highway 382 was completely lifted from its foundation, turned sideways, and moved more than 20 feet down the river.  Another log home nearby, also along Harris Branch Creek, was likewise removed from its foundation, at which point it crumbled apart as it was swept down the flooded creek.  One person, who was in the home at the time, rode the home down the river for nearly an hour before she was able to escape from a second story window with just minor injury.  The home was reduced to splinters shortly thereafter.  In Cartecay, the Cartecay Townhouses were flooded as well as 12 mobile homes in the Maple Village Mobile Home Park.  There were numerous reports of automobiles, trees, parts of homes, decks, and even appliances such as refrigerators being washed down the Coosawattee river toward Carters Lake.  Several people in the area had to be rescued from flood waters.  As many as 50 people had to take shelter in the Gilmer County Highschool.
The Jackson county 911 center reported that a couple of trees had been blown down.

A storm spotter, as well as the Gilmer county 911 center, reported that a number of trees and some power lines had been blown down in the southern areas of the county, particularly just south of Ellijay and along Georgia Highway 52 southeast of Ellijay.
Sheriff  reported 2 residences flooded on Jennifer Court.  Street flooding also occurred throughout the city.
The Dade county 911 center reported that a house was set on fire by lightning.
The Coweta county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Coweta county 911 center reported that a tree was blown down on a power line.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

The National Weather Service cooperative observer for Madison county reported that straight line thunderstorm wind gusts caused extensive damage to trees in the area.  Hundreds of trees were either uprooted or broken off, with extensive debris in the streets in and around Ila.  Several small structures in the area also sustained wind damage, mainly in the form of damage to roofs.  Pea-sized hail was also reported in the area.
A couple of trees were blown down.

The Dade county 911 center reported that power lines were down in five different areas of the county and a number of trees were blown down in eight different areas around the county.  The Emergency Management Director for Dade county estimated that at least 30 trees were blown down around the county.  An amateur radio operator reported that a 500 foot cell tower was also blown down.

The Walker County Messenger reported that literally dozens of trees were down on power lines and roads around the county.  The Rossville area was probably the hardest hit.  Seven homes were severely damaged when large trees fell on the structures, while at least 27 calls were received by the Walker county Fire and Rescue Department to remove trees blocking roads.  Several hundred residents of the county were left without power for several hours during the event.
The Troup county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down.
The public reported that several trees were blown down causing roof damage to a couple of structures and other minor property damage.
The Houston county 911 center reported that several trees were blown down.  The Macon Telegraph reported that the down trees and tree limbs resulted in a number of power outages in the county.
The public reported that a few trees were blown down on their property east of Miami Valley near Interstate 75.
The Forsyth county Assistant Emergency Management Director reported that a tree was blown down on a home off Pilgrim Mill Road causing minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, numerous power lines and a set of traffic lights were blown down.
Damage to an awning and the roof of the United Federal Credit Union on Gross Rd. and numerous trees down in Camden Way and Osprey Cove.
Quarter size hail reported at Mariner's Landing.
3.5 inches of rain fell between 1500-1600 at NWS employee's home.
Numerous trees and power lines down along County Road 15. Several large pecan trees were down and minor damage was caused to a home on Campbell Road.
Numerous large trees were blown down along County Road 203 near the Wayne and Appling County border
KVAX Doppler Radar indicated 5-7 inches of rainfall in a 3 hour period. Other radars in the area including KJAX and KCLX showed similar amounts.

Much of WFO Charleston's CWA was impacted by the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill as it tracked to the northeast across the extreme northern section of Georgia. The mini outbreak of tornadoes started around 8 p.m. on the night of July 1st and did not stop until after 115 a.m. on the morning of July 2nd. Fortunately, there were no deaths with the tornadoes. Screven County was hardest hit with three separate tornadoes and three areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines.

Screven County was hardest hit with three separate tornadoes and three areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado knocked down trees and damaged mobile homes

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of WFO Charleston's CWA was impacted by the remnants of Tropical Storm Bill as it tracked to the northeast across the extreme northern section of Georgia. The mini outbreak of tornadoes started around 8 p.m. on the night of July 1st and did not stop until after 115 a.m. on the morning of July 2nd. Fortunately, there were no deaths with the tornadoes. Screven County was hardest hit with three separate tornadoes and three areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

The Pickens county 911 center, as well as the public reported that a number of roads were flooded and impassable, especially around Ludville and Hinton.  Excessive rainfall of two to six inches in a three hour or less period had occurred.  At one location, at least one-half foot of water was reported to have been flowing across Georgia Highway 53 near Ludville.  The river gage on the Talking Rock Creek at Talking Rock rose eight feet in just 30 minutes.
The Gordon county 911 center reported that a number of trees were down across the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: NWS Co-Op Observer reported 3.5 inches of rainfall within several hours. Secondary roads were impassable and broken trees limbs damaged several homes along Nursery Road.
Secondary dirt and paved roads were washed out.
Okefenokee Rural Electric Membership Corporation reported 5 inches of rainfall accumulation during the late afternoon/early evening.
Okefenokee Rural Electric Membership Corporation reported 5 inches of rainfall accumulation during the late afternoon and evening.
Okefenokee Rural Electric Membership Corporation reported several county roads were flooded and impassable from recent heavy rain.
City Street Dept. reported some street flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Tech Police Department, as well as the Atlanta Police Department, local news media, and amateur radio operators, reported considerable flooding of streets throughout the downtown Atlanta area, including portions of the Interstate 75/85 Downtown Connector.  Some roads had to be briefly closed because of water flowing over the roads and there was at least one incident of a road collapse in the downtown area.  Afternoon rush hour traffic was brought to a standstill on the Interstate 75/85 Downtown Connector and Georgia Highway 166 (Lakewood Freeway/Langford Parkway).
The Wilkes county Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and some power lines were blown down along Georgia Highway 17 North between Tignall and Washington.
The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck a home on Meadow Walk Lane setting the attic on fire.  However, firefighters were able to minimize the damage from the fire.

The Barrow County News reported that lightning struck a pine tree adjacent to a home and set both the tree and the home on fire.  The second floor of the home was a complete loss and the first floor suffered extensive water damage.
The Pickens county 911 center reported penny-sized hail in Talking Rock and the public reported penny-sized hail in Ludville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management and Wayne County Road Dept. reported numerous secondary roads and streets were flooded and impassable. Water covered Gardi-Broadhurst Highway resulting in road closure.  Car was stranded on Gardi-Broadhurst Highway...no injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon county 911 center reported considerable flooding of roads in Calhoun.  Several downtown streets had at least one foot of water flowing over them.

The Gilmer county 911 center reported that Old Georgia Highway 5 was completely flooded in Ellijay with nearly a foot of water flowing over the road.  Several other highways in the county had places with six inches or more of water flowing over them.  Old Georgia Highway 5, along with several others in the county, had to be closed.
The Hall county 911 center reported that flash flooding had resulted in the washout of the Black Jack Road bridge.

The Forsyth county Assistant Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail.

Several reports of hail were received from the public, and one from an amateur radio operator.  The public reported penny-sized hail in Fayetteville and an amateur radio operator reported quarter-sized hail in Fayetteville.  The public reported quarter-sized hail five miles south-southeast of Fayetteville just of Georgia Highway 92 near Harp.

An off-duty National Weather Service (NWS) employee reported that lightning struck his house.  Damage was confined to electrical equipment, including a couple of computers, a light fixture, stove light switch control, and an electronic light timer.  No fire occurred.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
Two inch rainfall accumulations within an hour led to street and poor drainage area flooding.  Street flooding occurred at the intersection of Norwich and 1st street in Brunswick.

Golf ball to tennis ball sized hail reported by the general public, trained spotters and emergency management in and near the City of Homerville.  Total of four (4) separate reports in a 10 minute time frame.
The Cherokee county Emergency Management Director reported that several trees had been blown down.

The Pulaski county Emergency Management Director reported that a 14-year old male fell into a rain-swollen drainage ditch.  He was subsequently swept away and carried nearly two blocks away from where he fell in.  He was killed instantly.  Approximately 2.5 inches of rain had fallen in the area in just a 45 to 60 minute period of time.  High water was also reported on several roads in the area.
The Upson county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.

Thunderstorms dumped an estimated 4 to 6 inches of rain around high tide causing flooding in downtown Savannah.  Numerous streets and underpasses were closed due to flooding. Four people were rescued from their cars after driving into water that caused cars to stall.
Heavy rainfall flooded Denmark-Brooklet Road causing it to be closed.
Very heavy rain caused flooding which washed out Savannah Road and Spencer Road.  Highway 25 to the north of Millen was also flooded.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down large limbs.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees, several of which fell on cars.
A storm spotter reported quarter-sized hail.

The Upson county 911 center reported that excessive rainfall in a short period of time had resulted in flooding of several roads in the northwest part of the county with water flowing over the roads.  Roads flooded included Hendricks Church Road near Wilson's Farm Market, Molling, and Woodall Roads.
The Gilmer county 911 center reported that Georgia Highway 52 was flooded with several inches of water flowing over the road.
The Gilmer county 911 center reported that lightning struck a home near Walnut Mountain and set it on fire.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.

The Dahlonega Nugget reported that lightning struck a Poplar tree near a home on Ridge Point Road, setting an adjacent shed and the home on fire.  The home was in the Frogtown area of extreme eastern Lumpkin county near the White county line.  The home was a total loss.  Another lightning strike struck and killed a bull in a nearby pasture.

The News Observer of Blue Ridge reported that local thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the downtown area of Blue Ridge.  One house sustained damage when flood waters rose to the level of the window sills.  Flood waters also caused damage at the Sheriff's Office.  Furthermore, railroad tracks running through the area were also damaged by the flood waters.

The News Observer of Blue Ridge reported that lightning struck two people at the Sugar Creek Raceway during its first night of the racing season.  A 45-year old track announcer was injured when lightning struck the track's sound system and came through the microphone.  Some papers on the desk were also ignited during this incident.  In the stands, a fan was injured when he was struck by lightning.  Neither of the injuries were fatal.

A storm spotter for the National Weather Service (NWS) reported that strong straight-line thunderstorm winds, or a possible microburst or downburst, had resulted in considerable damage to a farm near the intersection of Dee Kennedy Road and Harmony Church Road in northwest Barrow county near the Gwinnett county line.  All together three to five structures were damaged or destroyed and some farm equipment was turned completely over.  Two barns were completely destroyed and the roof was torn off a chicken house.  The debris from the damage was scattered over a fairly large area.  Several trees were also blown down in the same area
The public reported that very heavy rain had resulted in some street flooding.

Local newspapers out of Winder reported a number of lightning related incidents that resulted in significant damage.  Two homes in Winder were struck by lightning, one on Lisa Circle, which suffered significant fire damage to an upstairs bonus room, while another home on Flanagan Mill Road suffered severe fire damage throughout.  A chicken house on Georgia Highway 124 in the far north part of the county was struck and burned completely, while a power pole on Georgia Highway 82 just east of Winder burned when it was struck by lightning.  No injuries or fatalities were reported during these incidents.

The Fayette county Emergency Management Director reported that Fayette County Medical and Fire Personnel responded to the lightning-related injury of a construction worker at the River's Edge Subdivision, just across the county border in Clayton county.  The worker was treated and released with minor injuries.
The Bibb county 911 center reported that some power lines were down on the west side of Macon.
The public reported that a Pine tree was blown down onto Georgia Highway 141, Peachtree Parkway, near Brookwood.
The public reported quarter-sized hail.
The public reported quarter-sized hail.
The Griffin Police Department reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: County road maintenance supervisor reported golfball size hail just south of Homerville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taliaferro county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Morgan county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down near the Golden Pantry.  Pea-sized hail was also reported.
The public reported quarter to half-dollar-sized hail.
The Walton county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
The Walton county 911 center reported that a tree was blown down.
The Greene county 911 center reported that lightning struck a building and set it on fire.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.
The public reported penny-sized hail.

The Newton county 911 center reported that several trees were down on Georgia Highway 212 near Stewart, one tree was down on Georgia Highway 36 near Stewart, one tree was down on Georgia Highway 11 near Mansfield, one tree was down on Georgia Highway 142 near Newborn, and yet another tree was down on Rutledge Road near the Morgan county line.

The Wilkinson County News reported that thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage, with trees blown down onto roads, bridges, railroad tracks, and power lines.  Several homes were severely damaged when large trees fell on or through the roofs of the homes.  A large tree was also blown down at the Unimin Corporation.  At least two roads were blocked because of downed trees.  Crews worked much of the night to restore electrical and phone service to the area and clean debris from the roadways.
A SkyWarn Spotter reported penny-sized hail.
The Greene county 911 center reported that several trees and power lines were down.

The public reported that thunderstorm winds caused minor property damage at one residence consisting of an awning blown off, six feet of facia siding on the roof edge peeled back, a shutter blown off, the top of one large tree and two smaller pear trees broken off, and a mailbox blown over.  Numerous other items were also scattered about in the lawn including lawn furniture, garbage cans, plants and planters, and a full gas grill cylinder which was moved 10 feet.
The public reported penny-sized hail.
The public reported hail ranging in size from quarters to slightly larger than half dollars.

The Upson county 911 center reported that persistent thunderstorms with heavy rain had resulted in considerable flooding in the Thomaston area.  Eleven roads had to be closed because of flooding, and one remained closed for a few hours even after the rain had ended.  Flood waters rose into several homes.  Three homes had to be evacuated because of flooding in a low-lying area of the city.  In addition, water was flowing over the dam of a small pond northwest of Thomaston, forcing the closure of the road below the dam.
The Newton county 911 center reported quarter-sized hail.
The Newton county 911 center reported that a house was set on fire.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.
The Newton county 911 center reported golf ball-sized hail.
The Jefferson county 911 center reported that lightning struck a house and set it on fire.  Damage estimates were inconclusive.
The public reported nickel to quarter-sized hail.  In one instance, the public reported that it hailed, mostly penny-sized, from six to eight minutes.
The public reported that very heavy rain resulted in brief flooding of streets and parking lots in the area.
Some trees and power lines were blown down.

The Flint Electric Membership Cooperative reported that some power lines were down along U.S. Highway 80 between Knoxville and the Bibb county line, near Sandy Point.  Several power outages were reported in the area as well.
The Greene county 911 center reported that several trees had been blown down throughout the county.
The Morristown, Tennessee National Weather Service (NWS) Forecast Office relayed a report they had received from the public of penny-sized hail.
The Troup county 911 center reported penny-sized hail on Georgia Highway 109 W near Abbottsford Road.
The Greene county 911 center reported penny-sized hail.
Emergency manager reported numerous trees down on power lines in downtown Homerville.
Heavy rainfall cause street flooding in poor drainage areas.  Estimates of 1.8 inches of rain in 30 minutes.

As much as 6 inches of rain fell across northern Elbert County during the morning hours, which resulted in significant flash flooding across the area by early afternoon. Portions of several roads were washed out, as were some culverts. Other roads were blocked for some time due to flooded creeks.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down large limbs along Highway 121 and 280 to the east of Reidsville.
Thunderstorm winds blew trees down across Lake View Road.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees and destroyed 2 barns.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down large limbs.

A strong storm system produced widespread heavy rainfall across the region, which resulted in some flash flooding across Habersham County. Ankle-deep water entered several businesses in the Baldwin and Cornelia areas. Some creeks overflowed their banks and flooded adjacent roads, including a portion of highway 105 that was washed away. Flooding developed as far north as Clarkesville.
Heavy rainfall caused Stecoah Creek to rise rapidly to flood south of Clayton. Another small creek flooded in Clayton, and a small pond overflowed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An uninhabited home on Yawn Cemetery Rd. in Hazelhurst was damaged by several downed trees and the roof was blown off.
Heavy rains washed out part of Pine Street near Buddy Spann Park and collapsed part of Hatton Still Road.

Heavy rainfall over several days caused numerous road closures across the county, including in the city of Jesup.  Here a road was washed out on Oglethorpe Landing, the rains eroded Jaycee Landing, and a house on Old Doctortown Road was flooded by standing water. Sandy Run Road, Maple Road and Beachwood Road were also closed in/near Jesup.  Wayne County Road Department reported that 30-40 roads countywide were closed because of wash outs by heavy rains.
Wayne County Recreation Director reported that heavy rains over the past week washed away hydroseed on a new Bill Morris park lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spalding county 911 center reported that a few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds (possible downburst) caused considerable damage to a residence in Madray Springs. A large oak was blown down, pine trees were snapped in half and the screened-in-patio mesh mesh wall was bent at a 45 degree angle with popped-out screens and supports. The family cat was "flung" across the yard and was found unconscious.

Heavy snow developed during the evening of the 18th, and continued into the morning of the 19th. Three to five inches of snow accumulated, mainly across the western and northern portions of the county.
Light snow, mixed at times with sleet, fell across the Georgia mountains, accumulating up to an inch in some areas.
Light snow, mixed at times with sleet, fell across the Georgia mountains, accumulating up to an inch in some areas.
Once the heavy snow ended, an extended period of light snow produced additional light accumulations across portions of the Georgia mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and power lines.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and large limbs.
Thunderstorm winds toppled several trees.
